rationale: The citing paper explicitly uses Thompson, Hamilton, and Rust (2005) to frame a key conceptual risk in its model: that choosing more feature-rich customized products could reduce satisfaction via "feature fatigue." It then reports analyses across studies on the link between feature richness and satisfaction, indicating the citation informs interpretation of results rather than serving as a purely generic background mention. Although the 2014 paper does not directly test the original before-vs-after-use mechanism from the cited work, it meaningfully leverages its core idea in a new customization-architecture context.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the feature-fatigue idea from Thompson et al. (2005) to the context of product customization architectures, using it to motivate and interpret tests of whether more selected features might lower satisfaction. The cited work functions as conceptual grounding for this potential downside while the new paper focuses on CvSS effects.
